1: vayolickheni ʾel-hashaʿar shaʿar ʾasher pone derekh haqqadim — the guide leads Ezekiel to the east gate. The east gate is the climax and destination of the entire architectural tour: all the measurements have been orientation-framework for the event that now occurs. Pone derekh haqqadim (facing the way of the east) repeats the phrase from 40:6 where the tour began at the east gate.
2: vehinneh kevod ʾElohei Yisraʾel ba midderekh haqqadim — "and behold the glory of the God of Israel came from the way of the east." The return of the kavod (glory) from the east is the inverse of its departure eastward to the Mount of Olives in 11:23. The kavod vocabulary is drawn from chs 1-3 and 8-11: qol (voice/sound) + mayim rabbim (many waters) is the theophanic sound-descriptor of 1:24; the earth shining with his glory (vehaʾarets hiʾirah mikkevodo) recalls the initial throne-chariot vision.
3: The vision Ezekiel now sees is like the vision at the time of the city's destruction (kimmarʾeh hammarʾeh ʾasher raʾiti bivoʾi leshaḥet ʾet-haʿir) and like the vision by the river Khebar. The divine self-consistency is the point: the kavod that judged Jerusalem is identical to the kavod that now returns to redeem it. YHWH does not change; the change is in the circumstances of encounter. Ezekiel falls on his face (vayyipol ʾal-panay) — the standard response to the theophany (1:28; 3:23).
4: ukhevod YHWH ba ʾel-habbayit derekh shaʿar ʾasher panayw derekh haqqadim — the glory of YHWH entered the house by the east-facing gate. The precision is theologically loaded: the same gate through which YHWH's glory departed (the Mount of Olives, 11:23) is the gate of return. The dwelling in the temple is not automatic but deliberate and directional: YHWH chooses the east gate as both the gate of departure and the gate of return.
5: vattiśśaʾeni ruach vatteviʾeni ʾel-heḥatser hapenimi — the Spirit (ruach) lifts and brings Ezekiel into the inner court. The Spirit-transport formula recurs from 2:2; 3:12, 14; 8:3; 11:1, 24 — each time Ezekiel is carried to a position of prophetic witness. The inner court is the closest a non-priest approaches the sanctuary; the Spirit brings Ezekiel there to witness the glory filling the house.
6: veʾeshmaʿ meiddabber ʾelai min-habbayit — Ezekiel hears the divine voice speaking from within the house. The shift from the angel-guide as speaker (throughout chs 40-42) to YHWH speaking directly (meiddabber ʾelai) marks the theological climax of the vision. The divine speech replaces the guided measurement tour — the sanctuary is now interpreted by its owner/inhabitant.
7: ben-ʾadam ʾet-meqom khisʾi veʾet-meqom kapot raglay — "son of man, this is the place of my throne and the place of the soles of my feet." The meqom khisʾi (place of my throne) combines the ark-throne and the footstool images from Ps 99:5; 132:7. Kapot raglay (soles of my feet) — YHWH's feet resting on the earth/temple echoes Isa 66:1. The forever-dwelling declaration — ʾasher eshkon sham benei Yisraʾel leʿolam — "where I will dwell among the children of Israel forever" — is the covenant-promise formula in its spatial form: the sanctuary as the locus of the mishkan (dwelling) relationship.
8: The specific sins that defiled the former temple — besimam sipam ʾet-sippi umezuzatam ʾetsel mezuzati — "by setting their threshold by my threshold and their doorpost beside my doorpost." The complaint: the royal palace shared a wall with the temple, symbolically treating the divine and human dwelling as co-equal. The royal construction that abutted the temple violated the qodesh/ḥol (holy/common) boundary that the entire vision of chs 40-42 has been establishing architecturally. Their toʿavot (abominations) defiled YHWH's holy name.
9: The removal of their harlotry and the carcasses of their kings — veʿattah yerraḥiqu ʾet-znutam veʾet-pigrê malkheyhem mimmenni — the condition for permanent divine dwelling: the ritual and political pollution must be removed. Pigrê malkheyhem (corpses/carcasses of their kings) may refer to royal burials adjacent to or within the temple precincts, a practice confirmed by archaeology. The promise: veshakhantti vetokhkham leʿolam (I will dwell among them forever).
10: veʾattah ven-ʾadam hagged ʾet-beit Yisraʾel ʾet-habbayit — "and you, son of man, describe the temple to the house of Israel." The purpose of the entire architectural vision is now stated: it is not only for Ezekiel to see but for him to describe to the exilic community. The act of description (hagged, hiphil of nagad, to declare/tell) carries the same weight as the act of measurement. Ezekiel's verbal declaration of the visionary temple shapes the community's hope.
11: The condition for the temple plan's disclosure to Israel — veʾim nikhlemu mikol ʾasher ʿasu tsurat habbayit — "if they are ashamed of all they have done, make known the form of the house." The nikhlem (ashamed, niphal of kalam, to be ashamed/humiliated) is the prerequisite for hearing the temple vision. The disclosure is conditional: the vision is given in anticipation of repentance, not despite its absence. Once ashamed, they are to know kol-tsurato (all its form/plan) and kol-ḥuqqotav (all its ordinances).
12: zot torat habbayit — "this is the law of the temple." The programmatic phrase torat habbayit (law/instruction of the house) is the title for the entire architectural-cultic revelation of chs 40-48. Torah here means not just "law" but "instruction/directive" — the blueprint for restored covenant life. The content of the law: ʿal-roʾsh hahar kol-gevulo saviv saviv qodesh qodashim — "on the top of the mountain, all its surrounding area shall be most holy." The entire mountain summit is declared qodesh qodashim (most holy/holy of holies) — the same superlative applied to the inner sanctuary's offerings (42:13). The mountain-top geography encodes graduated holiness at the landscape scale.
13: The altar measurements begin: veʾelleh middot hamizbeaḥ beʾammot — the altar in cubits. The base (ḥeq, literally bosom/hollow) one cubit high and one cubit wide with a rim (geshen) of a handbreadth — this is the lowest ledge of the altar. The technical term ḥeq hamizbeaḥ (the bosom of the altar) is unique here; it describes the base channel or drain of the altar that collects blood and liquids.
14: From the base (ḥeq haʾarets) to the lower ledge (ʾazarah haqqetanah): two cubits, one cubit wide; from the smaller ledge to the larger ledge (ʾazarah haggadolah): four cubits, one cubit wide. The stepped-ledge design of the altar — base, small ledge, large ledge, hearth — mirrors the stepped approach of the temple precincts: each step higher is more restricted, more holy.
15: vehaharʾel ʾarbaʿ ʾammot — the harʾel (hearth of God / altar-hearth, a hapax; possibly related to ʾariel of Isa 29:1, "hearth of God") is four cubits; from the ʾariel upward, the four horns (qarnot). The four horns of the altar are the most theologically charged feature: they are the objects to which the blood of sin offerings is applied (Exod 27:2; 29:12; Lev 4:7, 18, 25, 30) and to which one could flee for asylum (1 Kgs 1:50-51).
16: The harʾel (altar hearth) is twelve cubits square — ʾarbaʿ ʿasar ʾorek beʾarbaʿ ʿasar roḥav — actually fourteen cubits by fourteen, not twelve: the fourteen-by-fourteen measurement is the ʾariel upper surface. The square dimension with equal length and breadth (ʾarbaʿah tsedadim, on four sides) mirrors the Most Holy Place's 20 × 20 perfect square.
17: The larger ledge (ʾazarah haggadolah) fourteen cubits square — steps (maʿalot) facing east. Maʿalot (steps/ascent) from ʿalah (to go up/ascend) — the same root as the ʿolah (burnt offering, lit. that which goes up). The priest ascends by steps from the east to approach the altar top. The east-orientation of the altar steps parallels the east-orientation of the entire sanctuary.
18: veyyoʾmer ʾelai ben-ʾadam koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH ʾelleh ḥuqqot hamizbeaḥ — "he said to me: son of man, thus says Lord YHWH: these are the ordinances of the altar." The transition from measurement (middot) to ordinances (ḥuqqot) marks the altar section's movement from architecture to ritual. Ḥuqqot (statutes/ordinances) are divinely established ritual procedures — not arbitrary rules but the structuring principles of holy engagement.
19: The instructions for the altar's consecration day: give to the Levitical priests of the seed of Zadok (kizeraʿ Tsadoq) who approach YHWH a bull (par ben-baqar) as a sin offering (ḥattat). The Zadokite priesthood (bene Tsadoq) is Ezekiel's consistently preferred priestly line (40:46; 43:19; 44:15; 48:11) — the line that remained faithful when the Levites went astray. The specificity of the priestly lineage for the altar consecration is the cultic application of the general principle that holiness requires designated mediators.
20: The blood of the sin offering applied to the four horns (qarnot), the four corners of the ledge (ʾazarah ʾarbaʿ pinot), and the surrounding rim — vekhiparta ʿalav (and you shall make atonement for it). The verb kippar (piel: atone/purge) is the central cultic action: the altar must be atoned for before it can be used to make atonement. The seven-day altar consecration purges the altar from all defilement before regular worship begins.
21: The sin offering bull — velaqaḥta ʾet-happar haḥattat veserafto bamifqad habbayit miḥuts lammiqdash — burned at the appointed place outside the sanctuary (mifqad habbayit, the designated/appointed place of the house). The burning outside the sanctuary (not on the altar) follows the Levitical rule for sin offerings whose blood is brought into the holy place (Lev 4:12; 6:11; cf. Heb 13:11-13).
22: On the second day: a male goat without blemish (seʿir ʿizzim tamim) as a sin offering. The Levitical principle of tamim (without blemish, whole/complete) for sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3, 10; 3:1, 6; 4:3, etc.) is maintained. The goat's blood purges the altar as the bull's did: the two-day sin-offering consecration doubles the purification.
23: After completion of the purification — vekhaleta ḥatteʾ — a bull and a ram from the flock without blemish (pen-baqar tamim veʾayil min-hatsson tamim). The transition from sin offering (ḥattat) to burnt offering (ʿolah) marks the completion of the purification phase and the beginning of the consecration/dedication phase. The burnt offering is entirely consumed on the altar, expressing total dedication.
24: vehiqravtem lifnei YHWH — "and you shall present them before YHWH." The priests (kohanim) throw salt (melaḥ) on the offerings. The salt covenant (berit melaḥ, Num 18:19; Lev 2:13) — salt symbolizes incorruptibility and the endurance of the covenant relationship. Every offering is salted because every offering is covenant-ratifying.
25: Seven days — shivʿat yamim — a goat for a sin offering each day, and a bull and ram from the flock. The seven-day duration of the altar consecration parallels the seven-day ordination of the Aaronic priests (Lev 8:33-35) and the seven-day dedication of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:65). Seven days constitutes a complete time-unit for sacred transitions.
26: Seven days the priests make atonement (yekapperu) for the altar and cleanse it (vetiharu ʾoto) and consecrate it (umiluhu yado — lit. and fill its hand). The phrase millo yad (fill the hand) is the technical term for priestly ordination/installation, used throughout Exodus-Leviticus (Exod 28:41; 29:9, 29, 33, 35; Lev 8:33; etc.). Applying it to the altar is striking: the altar undergoes ordination as the priests do, because it is the primary locus of priestly service.
27: umimleʾot hayyamim vehaya vayom hashemiʾni vahalʾaah yaʿasu hakohanim ʾal-hamizbeaḥ — "and when the days are completed, it shall be that from the eighth day and onward the priests shall offer your burnt offerings and your peace offerings on the altar." The eighth day (yom hashemiʾni) is the day of new beginning — the day after the seven-day completion, the day of resurrection/new creation (also Lev 9:1; 12:3; the circumcision of the eighth day). Veratsti etchem — "and I will accept you." The declarative divine acceptance of the community through the altar service is the culminating point of the altar consecration: the restored sanctuary enables restored worship which enables YHWH's acceptance of his people.
